返回本批次

硬件领域的快速发展：从实验室到 1 亿美元 ARR 的经验教训

Moving fast in hardware: lessons from lab to $100M ARR

blog.zacka.io 作者 rryan 分数 97 评论 22 2026/04/07 23:07
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 当科林·查普曼说“简化，然后增加亮度”时，他指的是赛车。查普曼是一位传奇的 F1 工程师和路特斯汽车的创始人。他们赢得比赛不是通过增加动力，而是通过消除所有不承重的东西。他对重量如此着迷，以至于他的工程师开玩笑说，这些汽车的设计目的是在冲过终点线的那一刻就散架。他们中的一些人做到了。吉姆·克拉克 (Jim Clark) 驾驶科林·查普曼 (Colin Chapman) 的 Lotus 25 F1 赛车但这句话不仅仅意味着减轻重量。这是一种设计理念，几乎适用于复杂系统必须在约束下运行的每个领域——也就是说，所有工程和大部分公司建设。如果您从事机器人、航空航天、能源、国防、消费电子、医疗设备或汽车领域的开发，这就是为您准备的。我与他人共同创立了 ClearMotion，这是一家开发汽车机器人的公司，该机器人能够稳定车辆行驶和操控，我们将其从研究原型投入批量生产，ARR 超过 1 亿美元。我学到的重要教训之一是，硬件开发的速度不仅仅来自英勇的努力[1]。它来自于减少学习循环的质量。删除不必要的要求。折叠交接。将不确定性拉入内部。尽可能将复杂性从硬件转移到软件中。我犯了很多错误，拖慢了我们的步伐。以下是我来之不易的 6 个经验教训，讲述了构建物理产品的最佳团队如何快速行动，以及其他构建者做同样事情的故事。人们谈论硬件工程，就好像它本质上很慢一样。构建周期。工装。但导致进展缓慢的主要原因是管理跨学科的复杂性。需求负载、低效实验设计、跨职能距离、组织阻力。成功的团队通常是减分的团队。早期的主动悬架尝试，例如 Bose 的电磁系统和查普曼在 Lotus 的液压版本，旨在达到非常高的峰值力水平。从纸面上计算，这是正确的：在最大转弯时将两吨重的 SUV 保持平坦需要巨大的静力。但这一要求将您推向笨重、昂贵、耗电的架构。 Bose 花了数十年的时间来解决这个问题，但从未发货[2]。查普曼的系统增加了如此多的重量和复杂性，以至于与他自己的理念相矛盾。在 ClearMotion，我们做出的最重要的技术选择之一就是拒绝根据罕见的极端道路条件调整系统的尺寸。我们对数百辆汽车进行了测试，以研究实际的驾驶情况，不是教科书上的边缘情况，而是人们实际驾驶的方式，我们围绕这一现实进行设计。如今，我们的客户车辆车队每天都会收集数据，以更好地了解系统使用情况。结果：我们的峰值力需求约为其他人目标值的 20%。这一单一的减法打开了一个完全不同的设计空间。我们可以使用更简单的架构，这不仅意味着成本降低 90%，而且响应速度更快，从而为客户每天实际体验的活动带来更好的乘坐质量。我们能够删除许多组件，例如伺服阀、歧管、软管，并将大部分复杂性放入软件中。代价是，在罕见的边缘情况下，例如在 SUV 中进行激进的赛道驾驶，汽车将恢复到传统行为。客户没有注意到这一点，但每个人都注意到了这一点。最快的团队不仅仅在规范内进行优化。他们从第一原则的角度严格询问规范的哪些部分不是绝对必要的。由此可以实现惊人的工程速度。约翰·霍博尔特在黑板上提出了他的月球轨道交会概念。通过删除将整个航天器降落在月球上的要求，他使阿波罗计划成为可能。当美国宇航局的阿波罗计划选择如何登月时，该机构的大多数领导层都倾向于直接上升，将整个航天器降落在月球上并飞回家（明显的方法），或地球轨道交会，在出发前在轨道上组装一个大型飞行器。这两种方法都需要巨大的助推器和极高的登月能力。一位名叫约翰·霍博尔特 (John Houbolt) 的初级工程师确信，在指挥舱在轨道上等待的同时，发射一个小型专用着陆器降落在月球轨道上会效果要好得多。他是对的，但他必须绕过指挥系统，直接写信给副管理员，才能让这个想法得到认真对待。美国宇航局最终选择了他的方法。这是一个减法。通过取消将整个返回飞行器降落在月球表面的要求，他们消除了对更大助推器的需求，可以使用现有技术，简化热保护问题，并使时间表变得合理。如果没有这种结构上的减法，阿波罗几乎肯定不会在肯尼迪的最后期限前完成。你可以在 SpaceX 的航空电子设备中看到同样的本能。他们质疑了一个几十年来，业界一直认为这一要求是显而易见的：每台关键的飞行计算机都必须使用航天级组件。太空辐射硬化部件经过测试，符合每百万部件不到 1 次故障的标准，并且成本可能比商业同类产品高 100 倍到 1,000 倍。更糟糕的是，由于资格周期长，他们的表现往往落后一两代人。 SpaceX 取消了组件级别的要求，而是通过三重冗余投票架构来降低系统故障率，概念上类似于 RAID 阵列如何将不可靠的磁盘变成可靠的存储系统。其结果是成本低得多、迭代速度更快、零件可用性更广泛，并且能够以更接近商业世界的节奏升级计算。传统的航空航天方法优化了每件作品，力求完美。 SpaceX 优化了系统的弹性和开发速度。不同的规格，不同的设计空间。18年来，航空航天团队试图赢得人力飞行的克雷默奖，但大多数都以同样的方式失败：他们围绕一个无人质疑的隐含要求建造了精心设计的飞机：飞机不得损坏。这个要求似乎是显而易见的，但却是看不见的。但这迫使每个团队转向笨重、昂贵的设计，需要花费数月的时间才能建造，而当它们不可避免地在早期飞行中坠毁时，又需要数月的时间才能重建。每次失败的尝试都会让团队损失一年的时间。没有人能够以足够快的速度迭代学习。薄纱秃鹰在加利福尼亚州沙夫特飞行。 55 磅聚酯薄膜、铝管和胶带。旨在在数小时内崩溃并重建，优化迭代速度。加州理工学院航空学博士兼冠军滑翔机飞行员保罗·麦克雷迪删除了这一要求。他没有建造更好的飞机。他制作了一个一次性的。 Gossamer Condor 由聚酯薄膜、铝管、钢琴线和胶带制成。它重55磅。这看起来很荒谬。它不断崩溃。但当飞机坠毁时，他的团队可以在数小时内修复它并再次飞行，有时甚至在同一天内多次飞行。在开发过程中，秃鹰经历了 400 多次试飞和超过 12 次重大设计修改。他的竞争对手每年进行一到两次实验。通过删除未经审查的结构耐久性要求，他开辟了一个完全不同的设计空间，在这个空间中，学习速度比任何单次尝试的质量更重要。出发一年后，游丝秃鹰完成了近二十年来击败所有人的奖励课程。难的是，减法需要勇气。通常它需要对未知事物下注。添加要求似乎是安全的。但在早期的硬技术中，过度设计杀死的公司比设计不足的公司还要多。 Bose 的主动悬架在技术上非常出色，但在商业上却陷入了死胡同。从正确的规范开始。有时我从硬技术创始人那里得到的一个问题是我们如何构建我们的里程碑以及与之相关的筹款。我的一般答案是：设计一系列实验，其中每个实验都会消除下一个最重要的风险。尽早与客户互动，但对这些风险和时间表保持透明。寻找愿意参与这一旅程的投资者。向他们推销愿景、客户需求以及可信的计划和进展。最困难的部分是预测需要多少次迭代才能消除每个风险，这是我们多次犯错的。在开发新的硬技术时，早期原型不应该是微型生产单元。它们应该按照科学家设计实验的方式来设计：有明确的假设、受控变量，并诚实地解读结果。如果你的原型试图立即证明一切，它很可能无法证明任何令人信服的事情。Boom Supersonic 遵循严格的顺序。创始人 Blake Scholl 并不是从尝试对商用客机进行认证开始的，而是首先询问什么是真实的。该团队建造了三分之一规模的验证机 XB-1，以消除特定的技术未知数：他们能否设计出一种能够有效地将动能转换为压力的超音速入口？碳纤维复合材料能否承受持续超音速飞行的热载荷和结构载荷？数字稳定性增强系统能否使飞机在高马赫数下可控，而无需协和式飞机控制面的机械复杂性？测试计划中的每次飞行都旨在回答序列中的下一个问题——首先是亚音速处理，然后是跨音速行为，然后是超音速。 XB-1于2025年1月突破音障，成为第一架自主研发的民用喷气机。直到现在，Boom 才将这些经过验证的技术扩展到全尺寸客机 Overture 中。Boom 在莫哈韦沙漠上空的 XB-1 演示机。三分之一的规模，是为了在投入数十亿美元建造全尺寸客机之前消除特定的未知因素。NASA 的 X 飞机是最明确的这个想法的版本。 X-1、X-15 及其后继机型从未打算用于生产飞机。它们是技术演示器，旨在回答一两个具体问题：我们能否突破 1 马赫？控制面在 3 马赫时会发生什么？升力体可以重新进入大气层吗？并使答案足够清晰，以便工业界和军方采取行动。 X-plane 的理念是：不要建造事物，而要进行实验来告诉你这件事是否可能。这种理念也适用于产品策略。特斯拉最初的“总体规划”是经过深思熟虑的。他们一开始并不是试图制造一款廉价的大众市场电动汽车，这需要同时解决成本、制造规模、电池供应、品牌信任和产品需求。相反，Roadster 的设计目的是回答一个狭隘的问题：基于笔记本电脑电池的电池驱动汽车是否真正受欢迎？ Roadster 本质上是一辆采用 Lotus Elise 外壳的快速电动卡丁车。 Model S/X 测试了特斯拉是否可以制造“真正的”汽车，并由此产生正现金流。直到那时，他们才尝试 Model 3/Y，其中剩下的巨大风险是大批量制造。即使是这一单一风险，在 2017 年至 2019 年期间也几乎摧毁了该公司。但到那时，他们可以专注于这一主要事情。他们做到了。两年后，它们的价值达到一万亿美元。辉煌并没有进入低端市场。它对风险进行排序，以便在每个阶段都消除一个主要风险。特斯拉的总体规划被重新构建为实验设计。每辆车都是为了消除一种风险而制造的。对于初创公司来说，稀缺资源通常是金钱，但也可能是时间——而时间是你无法筹集更多资金的资源。麻省理工学院的衍生公司 Lilliputian Systems 筹集了 1.4 亿美元，用于开发丁烷驱动的燃料电池，作为手机和笔记本电脑的便携式充电器。当他们于 2001 年开始时，现有技术是镍镉和镍氢电池，它们的能量密度优势是有意义的（每体积能量比锂离子多五到十倍）。但他们试图并行解决所有问题：新颖的 MEMS 燃料电池制造工艺、在自身膨胀应力下破裂的膜的热管理、丁烷筒设计、交通部监管部门批准在飞机上携带燃料、消费品设计和零售分销。没有任何东西被测序。什么也没发货。多年来，他们基本上是秘密运作的，一再承诺“明年”就会推出产品。他们花了十多年的时间才推出了可交付的产品，此时锂离子电池已经取得了如此巨大的进步，并且价格下降了如此之多，以至于其价值主张已经消失殆尽。他们在 2014 年破产了。市场不会等你结束，而耗尽时间的最可靠方法就是尝试立即消除所有风险。自 X-plane 时代以来发生的一件事是，在投入硬件之前进行实验的成本有多低。高保真 CFD、FEA、多物理场求解器等现代仿真工具一直在这方面提供帮助，但高性能云计算和人工智能正在进一步压缩循环。根据模拟数据训练的代理模型可以比底层求解器更快地探索设计空间，从而使工程师可以在构建模型之前筛选数千个候选模型。基于物理的神经网络可以以传统曲线拟合无法做到的方式在稀疏测试数据点之间进行插值。这些都不能完全取代物理测试，但它可以更早地移动第一个过滤器，可以优化设计参数，并使每个物理原型更加了解情况。最优秀的技术团队在硬技术公司的早期阶段进行规划时就会内化这一点。他们问：我们不知道的下一件事情是什么？能够改变我们信念的最便宜的诚实模拟或测试是什么？我们在 ClearMotion 犯的一个错误是，在我们真正了解自己的流程之前，我们认为我们可以将制造外包给成熟的系统集成商。一方面，这吸引了我们的 OEM 客户，他们希望在生产线上看到一级供应商的名字。我们希望得到“以前做过这件事”的公司的支持。但另一方面，我们对装配流程以及如何确保每个步骤的质量还有很多不了解的地方。当供应商的官僚机构进行学习时，循环就会变得缓慢。幸运的是，我们有资源来学习这一教训并适应。我们在工程师所在的玻璃墙的另一侧建造了第一条生产线。如今，ClearMotion 为我们的客户拥有最终制造权，并且现在有了受控且易于理解的流程，随着世界各地新工厂的建立，外包系统组装变得可行。顺序很重要：首先理解它，然后可以考虑将其交出。一般的对位原则很简单。如果某物是商品：具有良好特征、成熟且不对于系统中的未知因素来说至关重要，那么购买通常会更快。 Mobileye 成功将其视觉处理芯片的生产外包给意法半导体。他们的 IC 制造工艺中几乎没有创新风险；创新在于 ASIC 设计和在其上运行的感知软件。这是一次彻底的分离，他们实现了 70% 的毛利率和快速增长，而边际资本支出几乎没有增加。[3]例如，对于 PCBA 组装，大多数流程也是标准化的。但是，如果一个系统直接位于您的核心不确定性之上，那么将其外包通常是您可以做出的最慢的选择。通常，对于复杂的硬技术系统，最终制造（至少在开始时）应该由您负责。起初，这对我来说是非常违反直觉的，因为高营业利润率、低资本支出生产外包的诱惑对习惯了硅谷方式的软件工程师很有吸引力。投资者喜欢我们原创的“轻资产”商业模式。我们逐渐了解到，唯一的问题是，当流程不确定时，效率会非常低。在复杂的机电一体化中，最终系统制造的早期样品和流程细化通常是工程流程本身的一部分。中国湖海军军械测试站的比尔·麦克莱恩领导的一个小团队开发了“响尾蛇”，成为有史以来最成功的空对空导弹之一。他们将制造、测试和设计迭代紧密地放在同一地点并处于自己的控制之下，刻意避免采用主承包商模式。传统的国防项目需要工程师编写详尽的规格，将其运送给承包商，并等待数月才能获得原型，而麦克莱恩的团队可以在早上画出一个想法，下午在自己的车间加工零件，并立即在飞行线上进行测试。工程师和技术人员之间没有等级之分——他们在相同的工作台和机床上并肩工作。一些最好的想法来自最接近金属的人。导弹的滚转稳定问题就是一个很好的例子。传统方法是电子系统：复杂、笨重且容易出现故障。一位名叫西德尼·克罗克特 (Sidney Crockett) 的技术人员提出了一种纯机械替代方案：安装在尾翼上的带缺口的小金属轮，这些轮子会在气流中旋转并充当被动陀螺仪，自动纠正任何滚动。这些“滚轮”比孤立工作的电子工程师设计的任何东西都更简单、更轻、更可靠。该解决方案只能在零件加工人员具有影响设计的地位和接近度的环境中出现。这个教训不仅仅是关于速度——在发明阶段，塑造设计的人和制造它的人应该是同一个团队。现代硬件中最有影响力的步骤之一是将性能从物理复杂性转移到软件和计算——使产品“软件定义”。当我和联合创始人 Shak 和我创办 ClearMotion 时，我们在流体系统中遇到了电机传感问题，一些经验丰富的供应商认为我们的系统架构无法解决这个问题。一名高级团队成员放弃并退出，写了一封信解释为什么这个问题在物理上无法解决。一个月后，我们开发了一种混合解决方案，该解决方案利用软件中的先进控制策略以及低保真度磁传感器。解决方案主要是计算性的。这段经历比其他任何事情都更影响了我对硬件和软件问题之间界限的看法。后来，我们将围绕软件定义硬件的原则设计系统，进行诸如持续强化学习来优化我们的控制算法、ML模型来改进低成本传感器信噪比、控制来彻底改变执行器动力学以及推出新功能等事情。如有疑问，请使用软件来实现。谷歌的数据中心在工业规模上提供了这一原理的示例。 2016 年，DeepMind 部署了深度神经网络来优化 Google 数据中心的冷却。这不是通过重新设计冷却器或气流架构来实现的，而是通过学习人类操作员手动管理的 120 多个物理变量（温度、泵速、阀门位置、天气条件）之间的非线性相互作用来实现。该系统仅使用现有的传感器。没有安装新硬件。结果是冷却能量减少了 40%，这是任何机械重新设计都无法实现的成果。到 2018 年，它已成为一个完全自主的控制系统，可以对物理工厂进行实时调整。这个教训与我们在传感问题中学到的教训是一样的：物理学没有改变，应用于物理学的智能改变了，而且由于架构允许用软件控制硬件，因此原子在无需任何人接触的情况下变得效率显着提高。一系列道路碎片撞击引发了人们对 Model S 电池组的安全担忧后，特斯拉推出了一项无线更新，可自动提高汽车在高速公路行驶时的行驶高度。这并不是召回安装新的防滑板（显而易见但昂贵的解决方案）。这是一个软件变更，在一夜之间通过空中部署，每辆车边际成本为零，它使用数字杠杆来解决物理世界的几何问题。软件定义的硬件具有纯物理系统无法比拟的售后弹性。好奇号的铝轮经过多年的火星地形考验。机械设计被锁定在 3.5 亿英里之外 - JPL 的修复方法是上传软件。 2013 年，NASA 意识到好奇号火星车上的铝轮受到锋利的火星岩石的灾难性损坏。机械设计被锁定，硬件在数百万英里之外自行撕裂。 JPL 工程师没有接受缩短的任务，而是开发了软件定义的物理解决方案。他们编写并上传了一种新的牵引力控制算法，该算法根据悬架的倾斜度和爬坡地形动态调整每个车轮的速度。该软件确保车轮和谐地推拉，大大减少了驱动车轮撞上尖锐岩石的纯粹物理力。 Code 有效地为流动站添加了保护性机械层，将其物理寿命延长了数年。其中许多创新需要在设计硬件时考虑到软件控制。内燃机传动系统本身无法以高分辨率对每个车轮施加扭矩矢量。但直驱电机可以。在 ClearMotion，我们的第一直觉通常是解决软件中的系统挑战。早期的原型由于泵几何形状引起的流体压力波动而产生噪音，但通过在软件中控制电机扭矩作为该几何形状的函数，我们主动消除了噪音。通过围绕软件控制设计系统，我们可以使用预测算法改变工厂动态，例如反射惯性。人工智能辅助控制系统设计可以根据实际数据同时调整数千个参数，找到手动校准需要数月才能达到的操作点。当我们需要高精度计算前方道路轮廓时，我们使用现有的车辆传感器数据来构建高保真道路高清地图，将其众包，根据该数据定位汽车，并根据无限预览控制行驶以减轻道路输入。我们是第一个在自动驾驶之外实现车辆控制强化学习的公司，使用深度学习框架，该框架可以随着时间的推移提高性能，并随着车辆、道路和硬件组件的变化进行调整。这些软件方法使我们的行动速度比物理重新设计快得多。里奇·萨顿（Rich Sutton）从人工智能研究中得出的“惨痛教训”在这里是一个松散的类比。他的观察是，在人工智能的历史上，利用计算的方法始终胜过利用人类设计的领域知识的方法。建造更多的结构、更多的手工制作的功能和巧妙的机制总是很诱人。但可扩展的方法是用通用计算取代定制复杂性的方法。我认为物理系统中正在发生类似的事情。最有可能获胜的团队是那些用更简单的硬件上的计算取代机械复杂性（定制连杆、特殊材料、附加传感器）的团队。因为软件是可迭代的。一个下午就可以更新模型。重新组装铸件需要几个月的时间。当您将控制权转移到软件时，迭代循环会更快 - 无论您是在减轻故障模式、添加功能还是调整性能。大野耐一（Taiichi Ohno）是丰田生产系统的架构师，他过去常常在工厂车间里用粉笔画圆圈，让工程师在里面站上几个小时，只是看着。重点不是纪律。这是带宽。站在流程旁边的工程师吸收书面报告中没有的信息（例如振动、时间、废物来源）。丰田通过 NUMMI（与通用汽车在加利福尼亚州弗里蒙特的一家工厂成立的合资企业）将这一理念带到了美国。几十年后，特斯拉收购了同一家工厂。在 Model 3 生产危机期间，埃隆·马斯克睡在生产线地板上，与团队一起解决每个第一瓶颈。他说：“我总是把办公桌移到问题最大的地方。”同样的建筑，同样的洞察力大野在 50 年前就已经编撰出来了。在 ClearMotion，我们聘请了一支由修补匠组成的早期团队，他们是专家工程师兼建造者，他们喜欢看到自己的创作变得栩栩如生。我们建造的每个办公室都有一个靠近我们办公桌的构建/测试实验室，或者有一个玻璃墙，我们的工程师可以在那里看到构建过程。我们与多家机械厂合作，大部分都在 2 小时车程范围内马萨诸塞州剑桥市，但越来越意识到即使这样也是有成本的：误解图纸、延迟交货、需要一周时间修复的质量缺陷、需要采购团队来管理供应商等等。一旦我们的资金能够支持，我们就在内部引入了广泛的机加工车间能力，这样我们的设计发布到零件可用的循环就可以在同一天而不是两周内完成。我们的行动速度更快。对于硬技术创始人来说，现实要简单得多：当你的工具制造商、工艺工程师、生产线集成商和工程师在周二下午都可以站在同一个工作台周围时，通常需要多次 Zoom 电话和 DHL 发货的问题可以在几个小时内得到解决。这远比廉价劳动力更重要，这是中国制造业主导地位背后的结构性秘密。它们的最终优势是无与伦比的密度。当我们在上海郊外建造一座棕地工厂时，我们几乎能够在当地采购所有组件。我们的生产线集成商是本地的。我们聘请了经验丰富、工作勤奋的员工，他们之前曾与这些供应商（和 OEM 客户）一起推出过产品。我们在 6 个月内完成了在美国花了 24 个月的时间（我们必须与分布式供应商、集成商和合作伙伴合作）。如果您希望您的硬件团队快速行动，您必须积极压缩物理学习循环。工程师与产品的物理现实（构建、测试、生产）之间的每一英里距离都会对您的速度造成负担。轻松的最终形式是组织。团队中的沟通开销大致以人员数量的平方增长。在硬件中，工件是物理的、多模式的、深度特定领域的，并且无法清晰地进行差异化或版本控制，这成为速度的主要瓶颈。传奇般的快速和雄心勃勃的洛克希德·马丁臭鼬工程部门的运作原则是：使用正常程序将使用的人员数量的 10-25%。 SR-71、U-2、F-117 都是由按照航空航天标准衡量的小型团队制造的。 Skunk Works 的领导者 Kelly Johnson 有 14 条规则，其中有几条本质上是为了让团队足够小，以便每个人默认共享上下文。我们在 ClearMotion 看到了这一点。当我们的人数超过大约三十人时，我们的生产力明显下降，此时我们不得不引入更多的孤岛，更多的劳动分工，而且我们在身体上已经不再适合在一个房间里了。上下文共享变得更慢。这就是人工智能对硬件工程最被低估的影响——不是生成设计，而是压缩信息开销，迫使团队首先要做大。轻盈也是一种心态。我们犯下的最昂贵的招聘错误不仅仅是雇佣了“错误的人”，而且还雇佣了那些不相信事情可以通过其他方式完成的人。早期，我们可以用一个小团队在多个客户项目中流畅地执行大量工作，因为我们不知道更多。我们不知道在 1 级，他们有 50 个人做 OEM 应用程序工作，而我们只有 5 个人做。但当我们聘请了更有经验的工程经理时，一些人主张扩大团队、增加设备和资源、降低风险。他们没有想到我们如何能够以更少的成本可靠地完成工作。再加上我们有一个九位数的银行账户，可以满足这些请求，这导致了超支。流程很有用（事实上，我们更大的应用程序团队减少了最后一刻的消防演习）。但过程也是质量的。正确的问题不是你是否有流程。关键在于您的流程是否帮助系统更快地学习。最好的硬件团队行动迅速，因为他们减少了学习循环的质量。他们删除不重要的要求。他们构建能够回答一个问题的原型。他们将成熟的外包出去，将不确定的外包出去。他们用软件代替了可以避免的物理复杂性。他们将设计、构建、测试和生产紧密结合在一起。他们让团队规模足够小，以便共享背景。他们越来越多地使用人工智能工具来保持这些循环的快速运行，即使系统的复杂性不断增加。简化，然后增加亮度。查普曼谈论的是赛车，但他描述的是更普遍的东西：在约束下快速行驶。每个硬件公司的运营都受到限制。下次当你的硬件程序感觉很慢时，不要问“我们怎样才能跑得更快。”问“我们携带了什么我们不需要的东西。”[1] 虽然像带着装满执行器的 Pelican 箱子前往德国的当日票这样的英雄事迹是获得星空联盟金牌的好方法[2] 我们最终为他们的才华横溢的团队收购了它们并控制了知识产权[3] Mobileye 是在增长和业务战略方面建立汽车科技公司最成功的故事之一[4] 据报道，第一个原型是在麦克莱恩的私人车库

Original Summary

  1. When Colin Chapman said “simplify, then add lightness,” he was talking about racecars. Chapman was a legendary F1 engineer and founder of Lotus. They won races not by adding power but by removing everything that wasn’t load-bearing. He was so obsessive about weight that his engineers joked the cars were designed to fall apart the moment they crossed the finish line. Some of them did. Jim Clark driving Colin Chapman’s Lotus 25 F1 racecarBut the phrase means more than weight savings. It’s a design philosophy that maps onto almost every domain where complex systems must perform under constraint — which is to say, all of engineering, and most of company-building. If you’re building in robotics, aerospace, energy, defense, consumer electronics, medical devices, or automotive, this is for you.I co-founded ClearMotion, a company developing automotive robotics that stabilize vehicle ride and handling, and we took it from a research prototype into volume production and >$100M ARR. One of the important lessons I learned is that speed in hardware development doesn’t just come from heroic effort[1]. It comes from reducing the mass of the learning loop. Delete unnecessary requirements. Collapse handoffs. Pull uncertainty inside. Push complexity from hardware into software where you can. I made a lot of mistakes that slowed us down. Here are my top 6 hard-earned lessons about how the best teams building physical products can move fast, along with stories of other builders doing the same.People talk about hardware engineering as if it’s slow by nature. Build cycles. Tooling. But most of what makes it slow is managing cross-disciplinary complexity. Requirement loads, inefficient experimental design, cross-functional distance, organizational drag. Teams that succeed are usually the ones that subtract.Earlier attempts at active suspension such as Bose’s electromagnetic system and Chapman’s own hydraulic version at Lotus aimed at very high peak force levels. On paper it’s correct when you run the math: holding a two-ton SUV flat during max cornering requires enormous static force. But that requirement pushes you toward heavy, expensive, power-hungry architectures. Bose spent decades on the problem and never shipped[2]. Chapman’s system added so much weight and complexity that it contradicted his own philosophy.At ClearMotion, one of the most important technical choices we made was refusing to size the system for the rare, extreme on-road condition. We instrumented hundreds of cars to study actual driving profiles, not textbook edge cases, but how people actually drive and we designed around that reality. Today our fleet of customer vehicles collect data every day to better understand system usage. The result: our peak force requirement was ~20% of what others had targeted. That single subtraction opened a completely different design space. We could use a simpler architecture, which meant not just 90% lower cost but also much faster response, which resulted in better ride quality for the events customers actually experience every day. We were able to delete a number of components such as servovalves, manifolds, hoses and push most complexity into software. The tradeoff was that in a rare edge case such as aggressive track driving in an SUV, the car would revert to conventional behavior. Customers didn’t notice that, but everybody noticed the ride.The fastest teams don’t merely optimize within the spec. They rigorously interrogate which parts of the spec aren’t absolutely necessary from a first principles perspective. A surprising amount of engineering speed can come from this.John Houbolt at the blackboard with his lunar-orbit rendezvous concept. By deleting the requirement to land the entire spacecraft on the moon, he made Apollo’s schedule possible.When NASA’s Apollo program was choosing how to get to the moon, most of the agency’s leadership favored either direct ascent, landing the entire spacecraft on the moon and flying it home (the obvious approach), or Earth-orbit rendezvous, assembling a large vehicle in orbit before heading out. Both approaches required a colossal booster and extreme lunar landing capability. A relatively junior engineer named John Houbolt became convinced that lunar-orbit rendezvous, sending a small, purpose-built lander down while the command module waited in orbit, was dramatically better. He was right, but he had to go around the chain of command, writing directly to the associate administrator, to get the idea taken seriously. NASA eventually chose his approach. It was a subtraction. By removing the requirement to land the entire return vehicle on the lunar surface, they eliminated the need for a much larger booster, could use existing technologies, simplified the thermal protection problem, and made the schedule plausible. Without that architectural subtraction, Apollo almost certainly would not have made Kennedy’s deadline.You can see the same instinct in SpaceX’s avionics. They questioned a requirement the industry had treated as obvious for decades: every critical flight computer must use space-grade components. Space radiation-hardened parts are tested to standards like less than one failure per million parts and can cost 100x to 1,000x more than commercial equivalents. Worse, they’re often a generation or two behind in performance because of the long qualification cycles. SpaceX removed the requirement at the component level and instead designed for low system failure rates through triple-redundant voting architectures, conceptually similar to how a RAID array makes unreliable disks into a reliable storage system. The result was much lower cost, faster iteration, broader part availability, and the ability to upgrade compute on a cadence closer to the commercial world. The heritage aerospace approach optimized each piece for perfection. SpaceX optimized the system for resilience and speed of development. Different spec, different design space.For 18 years, aerospace teams tried to win the Kremer Prize for human-powered flight, most failing in the same way: they built carefully engineered aircraft around an implicit requirement no one questioned: the aircraft must not break. That requirement seemed so obvious it was invisible. But it pushed each team toward heavy, expensive designs that took months to build, and when they inevitably crashed on early flights, it took months to rebuild. Each failed attempt cost teams a year. No one could iterate fast enough to learn.The Gossamer Condor in flight at Shafter, California. 55lb of Mylar, aluminum tubing, and tape. Built to crash and rebuild in hours, optimizing for iteration speed.Paul MacCready, a Caltech aeronautics PhD and champion glider pilot, deleted that requirement. He didn’t build a better aircraft. He built a disposable one. The Gossamer Condor was made of Mylar film, aluminum tubing, piano wire, and tape. It weighed 55 pounds. It looked absurd. It crashed constantly. But when it crashed, his team could repair it in hours and fly again, sometimes multiple times in the same day. Over the course of development, the Condor went through more than 400 test flights and over 12 major design modifications. His competitors ran one or two experiments per year. By deleting the unexamined requirement of structural durability, he opened a completely different design space, one where the rate of learning mattered more than the quality of any single attempt. A year after starting, the Gossamer Condor completed the prize course that had defeated everyone else for nearly two decades.The hard part is that subtraction requires courage. Often it requires a bet on the unknown. Adding requirements seems safe. But in early-stage hard tech, overdesign kills more companies than under-design. Bose’s active suspension was technically extraordinary but a commercial dead-end. Start with the right spec.One question I sometimes get from hard-tech founders is how we structured our milestones, and the fundraises attached to them. My general answer is: design a sequence of experiments where each one retires the next most important risk. Engage customers early but be transparent about those risks and the timeline. Find investors who buy into that journey. Sell them on the vision, customer demand, and the credible plan and progress therein. The hard part, something we got wrong on several occasions, is predicting how many iterations are needed to burn down each risk.When developing a new hard technology, early prototypes should not be miniature production units. They should be designed the way a scientist designs an experiment: with a clear hypothesis, controlled variables, and an honest read on what the result tells you. If your prototype is trying to prove everything at once, it will likely prove nothing convincingly.Boom Supersonic followed a disciplined sequence. Founder Blake Scholl didn’t start by trying to certify a commercial airliner, he started by asking what needed to be true first. The team built XB-1, a one-third-scale demonstrator, to retire specific technical unknowns in order: Could they design a supersonic inlet that efficiently converts kinetic energy to pressure? Could carbon-fiber composites hold up under the thermal and structural loads of sustained supersonic flight? Could a digital stability augmentation system keep the aircraft controllable at high Mach numbers without the mechanical complexity of Concorde’s control surfaces? Each flight in the test program was designed to answer the next question in the sequence — subsonic handling first, then transonic behavior, then supersonic. XB-1 broke the sound barrier in January 2025, the first independently developed civil jet to do so. Only now is Boom scaling those validated technologies into Overture, the full-size airliner.Boom’s XB-1 demonstrator over Mojave. One-third scale, built to retire specific unknowns before committing billions to the full-size airliner.NASA’s X-planes were the most explicit version of this idea. The X-1, X-15, and their successors were never intended as production aircraft. They were technology demonstrators, built to answer one or two specific questions: Can we break Mach 1? What happens to control surfaces at Mach 3? Can a lifting body reenter the atmosphere? and to make the answers legible enough for industry and the military to act on. The X-plane philosophy was: don’t build the thing, build the experiment that tells you whether the thing is possible.This philosophy scales to product strategy too. Tesla’s original “master plan” was deliberate. They didn’t begin by trying to build an inexpensive mass-market EV, which would have required solving cost, manufacturing scale, battery supply, brand trust, and product desirability simultaneously. Instead, the Roadster was designed to answer a narrow question: can a battery-powered car based on laptop cells be genuinely desirable? Roadster was essentially a fast electric go-kart in a Lotus Elise shell. The Model S/X tested whether Tesla could manufacture a “real” car, and generate positive cash flow doing so. Only then did they attempt the Model 3/Y, where the remaining big risk was high-volume manufacturing. Even that single risk nearly destroyed the company between 2017 and 2019. But by then they could focus on that one main thing. And they did. Two years later, they were worth a trillion dollars. The brilliance was not moving downmarket. It was sequencing the risks so that at each stage, they were burning down one main risk.Tesla’s master plan reframed as experimental design. Each vehicle was built to retire one risk.For startups, the scarce resource is usually money, but it can also be time — and time is the one you can’t raise more of. MIT spinout Lilliputian Systems raised $140 million to develop butane-powered fuel cells as portable chargers for phones and laptops. When they started in 2001, the incumbent technology was NiCad and NiMH batteries, and their energy density advantage was meaningful (five to ten times more energy per volume than lithium-ion). But they tried to solve everything in parallel: a novel MEMS fuel cell fabrication process, thermal management of a membrane that cracked under its own expansion stress, butane cartridge design, DOT regulatory approval to carry fuel on aircraft, consumer product design, and retail distribution. Nothing was sequenced. Nothing shipped. They operated largely in secret for years, repeatedly promising a product “next year.” It took them over a decade to reach a shippable product, by which point lithium-ion batteries had improved so dramatically, and dropped so far in price, that the value proposition had evaporated beneath them. They went bankrupt in 2014. The market doesn’t wait for you to finish, and the surest way to run out of time is to try to retire every risk at once.One thing that’s changed since the X-plane era is how cheaply you can run experiments before committing to hardware. Modern simulation tools like high-fidelity CFD, FEA, multiphysics solvers have always helped here, but high performance cloud compute and AI is compressing the loop further. Surrogate models trained on simulation data can explore a design space orders of magnitude faster than the underlying solver, letting engineers screen thousands of candidates before building one. Physics-informed neural networks can interpolate between sparse test data points in ways that traditional curve-fitting can’t. None of this completely replaces physical testing, but it moves the first filter earlier, can optimize design parameters, and makes each physical prototype more informed. The best technical teams planning the early innings of a hard-tech company internalize this. They ask: what is the next thing we don’t know, and what is the cheapest honest simulation or test that would change our beliefs?A mistake we made at ClearMotion was believing we could outsource manufacturing to established system integrators before we truly understood our own process. On one hand, this appealed to our OEM customers who wanted to see a Tier-1 supplier’s name on the manufacturing line. We wanted the support of companies that “had done it before.” But on the other hand, there was an enormous amount we didn’t yet know about our assembly process and how to ensure quality at each step. When that learning happens through a supplier’s bureaucracy, then the loop slows to a crawl.Luckily, we had the resources to learn this lesson and adapt. We built our first production line on the other side of a glass wall from where our engineers sat. Today ClearMotion owns final manufacturing for our customers, and now that there is a controlled, well-understood process, outsourcing system assembly is feasible as new factories are build around the world. The sequence matters: understand it first, then one can consider handing it off.The general counterpoint principle is straightforward. If something is a commodity: well-characterized, mature, and not central to the unknowns in your system, then buying it is usually faster. Mobileye successfully outsourced production of their vision-processing chips to STMicroelectronics. There was very little innovation risk in their IC fabrication process; the innovation was in the ASIC design and the perception software running on it. That’s a clean separation, and for them enabled 70% gross margins and rapid growth with little marginal capex increase.[3] For PCBA assembly, for example, most processes are also standardized. But if a system sits directly on top of your core uncertainties, outsourcing it is often the slowest choice you can make. More often than not, for complex hard-tech systems, final manufacturing, at least at first, should be your responsibility. This was incredibly counterintuitive to me at first, as the siren call of high operating margin, low capex production outsourcing is appealing to a software engineer accustomed to the ways of Silicon Valley. Investors loved our original “asset light” business model. The only problem, as we came to learn, was that it’s incredibly inefficient when the process is uncertain.In complex mechatronics, early samples and process refinement for final system manufacturing is often part of the engineering process itself.A small team led by Bill McLean at the China Lake Naval Ordnance Test Station developed what became one of the most successful air-to-air missiles ever built, the Sidewinder. They kept fabrication, testing, and design iteration tightly co-located and under their own control, deliberately avoiding the prime-contractor model. Where a traditional defense program would have engineers writing exhaustive specs, shipping them to a contractor, and waiting months for a prototype, McLean’s team could sketch an idea in the morning, machine the parts in their own shop that afternoon, and test it on the flight line immediately. There was no hierarchy between engineers and technicians — they worked side by side at the same benches and machine tools. Some of the best ideas came from the people closest to the metal. The missile’s roll-stabilization problem is a perfect example. The conventional approach was an electronic system: complex, heavy, and failure-prone. A technician named Sidney Crockett suggested a purely mechanical alternative: small notched metal wheels mounted on the tail fins that would spin in the airflow and act as passive gyroscopes, automatically correcting any roll. These “rollerons” were simpler, lighter, and more reliable than anything an electronics engineer working in isolation would have designed. That solution could only emerge in an environment where the person machining the parts had the standing and proximity to influence the design. The lesson wasn’t just about speed — it was that during the invention phase, the people shaping the design and the people fabricating it should be the same team.One of the most impactful steps in modern hardware is shifting performance from physical complexity into software and computation—to make products “software defined.”When my co-founder Shak and I started ClearMotion we had a motor sensing problem in a fluid system that several experienced suppliers concluded was unsolvable with our system architecture. One senior team member gave up and quit, writing a letter about why the problem was physically impossible to solve. A month later we had developed a hybrid solution that utilized an advanced control strategy in software, along with a low fidelity magnetic sensor. The solution was mostly computational. That experience shaped how I think about the boundary between hardware and software problems more than almost anything else. Later we would design the system around this principle of software-defined hardware, doing things such as continuous reinforcement learning to optimize our control algorithms, ML models to improve low-cost sensor signal-to-noise, controls to completely change the actuator dynamics, and launch new features. When in doubt, do it with software.Google’s data centers offer an example of this principle at industrial scale. In 2016, DeepMind deployed a deep neural network to optimize cooling across Google’s data centers. This was achieved not by redesigning the chillers or airflow architecture, but by learning the nonlinear interactions between more than 120 physical variables (temperatures, pump speeds, valve positions, weather conditions) that human operators had been managing by hand. The system used only existing sensors. No new hardware was installed. The result was a 40% reduction in cooling energy, gains that no amount of mechanical re-engineering could achieve. By 2018 it was a fully autonomous control system making real-time adjustments to the physical plant. The lesson is the same one we learned with our sensing problem: the physics didn’t change, the intelligence applied to the physics did, and because the architecture allowed for the hardware to be controlled with software, the atoms became dramatically more efficient without anyone touching them.After a series of road-debris strikes raised safety concerns about the Model S battery pack, Tesla pushed an over-the-air update that automatically raised the car’s ride height at highway speeds. It wasn’t a recall to install new skid plates (the obvious but expensive solution). It was a software change, deployed over the air, overnight, at zero marginal cost per vehicle and it used a digital lever to solve a physical world geometry problem. Software-defined hardware has post-sale elasticity that physical-only systems can’t match.Curiosity’s aluminum wheels after years of Martian terrain. The mechanical design was locked in 350 million miles away — JPL’s fix was a software upload.In 2013, NASA realized the aluminum wheels on the Mars Curiosity Rover were taking catastrophic damage from sharp Martian rocks. The mechanical design was locked in, and the hardware was tearing itself apart millions of miles away. Instead of accepting a shortened mission, JPL engineers developed a software-defined physical solution. They wrote and uploaded a new traction control algorithm that dynamically adjusted the speed of each individual wheel based on the suspension’s tilt and the terrain it was climbing. The software ensured the wheels pushed and pulled in harmony, drastically reducing the sheer physical forces driving the wheels into sharp rocks. Code effectively added a protective mechanical layer to the rover, extending its physical life by years. Many of these innovations require hardware designed with software control in mind. A combustion engine drivetrain can’t natively torque vector each wheel at high resolutions. But direct drive electric motors can.At ClearMotion, our first instinct is usually to solve system challenges in software. Early prototypes were noisy due to fluid pressure fluctuations caused by pump geometry, but by controlling motor torque as a function of that geometry in software, we actively canceled the noise. By designing the system around software control, we could alter plant dynamics such as reflected inertia using predictive algorithms. AI-assisted control system design can tune thousands of parameters simultaneously against real-world data, finding operating points that manual calibration would take months to reach. When we needed to calculate forward road contour with high accuracy, we used existing vehicle sensor data to build high-fidelity HD maps of the road, crowdsourced them, localized cars against that data, and controlled the ride to mitigate road input based on infinite preview. We were the first to implement reinforcement learning for vehicle control outside of automated driving, using a deep learning framework that improves performance over time and adapts as the vehicle, road, and hardware components change. These software approaches allowed us to move much faster than physical redesigns.Rich Sutton’s “Bitter Lesson” from AI research is a loose analogy here. His observation is that over the history of AI, methods that leverage computation have consistently won out over methods that leverage human-engineered domain knowledge. The temptation is always to build in more structure, more hand-crafted features, clever mechanisms. But the approaches that scale are the ones that replace bespoke complexity with general computation. I think something analogous is happening in physical systems. The teams best poised to win are the ones that replace mechanical complexity — bespoke linkages, exotic materials, additional sensors — with compute over simpler hardware. Because software is iterable. You can update a model in an afternoon. Retooling a casting takes months.When you shift control authority into software, iteration loops are faster—whether you’re mitigating failure modes, adding functionality, or adapting performance.Taiichi Ohno, architect of the Toyota Production System, used to draw chalk circles on factory floors and make engineers stand inside them for hours, just watching. The point wasn’t discipline. It was bandwidth. An engineer standing next to the process absorbs information (e.g. vibration, timing, sources of waste) that isn’t in a written report. Toyota brought this philosophy to the United States through NUMMI, a joint venture with GM at a plant in Fremont, California.Decades later, Tesla bought that same factory. During the Model 3 production crisis, Elon Musk was sleeping on the production line floor, working with the team to solve each #1 bottleneck. He said, “I always move my desk to wherever the biggest problem is.” Same building, same insight Ohno had codified fifty years earlier.At ClearMotion we hired an early team of tinkers, expert engineer-builders who liked to see their creations come alive. Each office we built had a build/test lab adjacent our desks or with a glass wall where our engineers could see builds happening. We worked with several machine shops, mostly within a 2 hour drive of Cambridge, MA, but increasingly realized that even that had costs: misunderstood drawings, late deliveries, quality defects that took a week to fix, the need for a procurement team to manage suppliers, etc. As soon as our financing could support, we brought extensive machine shop capabilities in house so our design-release to parts-available loop could be same day instead of two weeks. We moved faster.For a hard-tech founder, the reality is much simpler: when your toolmaker, process engineer, line integrator, and engineers can all physically stand around the same workbench on a Tuesday afternoon, problems that usually take multiple Zoom calls and DHL shipments are solved in hours.This, far more than inexpensive labor, is the structural secret behind China’s manufacturing dominance. Their ultimate advantage is unparalleled density. When we built a brownfield factory outside of Shanghai we were able to source almost every component locally. Our line integrator was local. We hired experienced, hard-working staff that had launched products with these suppliers (and OEM customers) before. We accomplished in 6 months what took us 24 months in the USA (where we had to work with distributed suppliers, integrators and partners).If you want your hardware team to move fast, you have to aggressively compress physical learning loops. Every mile of distance between engineers and the physical reality of the product (build, test, produce) is a tax on your speed.The final form of lightness is organizational.Communication overhead in a team grows at roughly the square of the number of people. In hardware, where artifacts are physical, multimodal, deeply domain specific, and can’t be diff’d or version-controlled as cleanly, this becomes a major bottleneck to speed.The legendarily fast and ambitious Lockheed Martin Skunk Works division operated on the principle: use 10-25% of the headcount that a normal program would use. The SR-71, the U-2, the F-117 were all built by teams that were tiny by aerospace standards. The Skunk Works leader Kelly Johnson had fourteen rules, and several of them were essentially about keeping the team small enough that everyone shared context by default.We saw this at ClearMotion. We experienced a noticeable drop in productivity when we grew beyond roughly thirty people, at which point we had to introduce more silos, more division of labor, and we physically didn’t fit in one room anymore. Sharing of context became slower. This is where AI might have its most underrated impact on hardware engineering— not generating designs, but compressing the informational overhead that forces teams to get big in the first place. Lightness is also a state of mind. The most expensive hiring mistakes we made were not just hiring the “wrong people,” it was hiring people who didn’t believe things could be done another way. Early on, we could execute an extraordinary amount of work with a tiny team moving fluidly across multiple customer programs because we didn’t know any better. We didn’t know that at a Tier-1 they’d have 50 people doing the OEM applications work we had 5 people doing. But as we hired more experienced engineering managers, several advocated for larger teams, more equipment and resources, less risk. They didn’t imagine how we could credibly do the work with less. Couple that with the fact that we had a nine-figure bank account and could accommodate these requests, and it was a recipe for overspending.Process is useful (indeed, our larger applications teams resulted in less last-minute fire drills). But process is also mass. The right question is not whether you have process. It’s whether your process is helping the system learn faster.The best hardware teams move fast because they reduce the mass of the learning loop. They delete requirements that don’t matter. They build prototypes that answer one question. They outsource the mature and insource the uncertain. They substitute software for avoidable physical complexity. They keep design, build, test and production close together. They keep teams small enough to share context. And increasingly, they use AI tools to keep those loops fast even as the system scales in complexity.Simplify, then add lightness. Chapman was talking about racecars, but he was describing something more general: going fast under constraint. Every hardware company operates under constraint. The next time your hardware program feels slow, don’t ask ‘how do we go faster.’ Ask ‘what are we carrying that we don’t need.’[1] Although heroics like same day tickets to Germany with Pelican cases full of actuators is a good way to get Star Alliance Gold[2] We ended up acquiring them for their talented team and controls IP[3] Mobileye is one of the greatest success stories of building an automotive tech company in terms of growth and business strategy[4] The first prototype was reportedly built in McLean’s personal garage

评论（默认中文）

syntaxing 2026/04/08 00:47
读起来很有趣，但有点太多废话了？我从事 MechE 设计已有大约十年的时间，但我绝不像 OP 那样成功。但我曾在一个 5 人的设计团队工作过，年净收入为 3000 万美元，而在另一份工作中，我与当时只有 2 个人（我和我的导师）的团队签订了 2500 万美元以上的合同。归根结底，硬件执行很困难，并且有大量的产品开发周期理论，您只需要一种具有我所说的良好工程纪律的文化。良好的沟通、良好的文档以及具有责任感的现实里程碑。
Fun read but a bit too much fluff? I was a design MechE for about a decade and I’m by no means as successful as the OP. But I have worked on a 5 person design team that had an annual net revenue of 30M and another job where I worked on 25M+ contract with just a team of 2, me and my mentor at the time. End of the day, hardware execution is hard and there’s a ton of product development cycle theories, you just need a culture with what I call good engineering discipline. Good communication, good documentation, and realistic milestones with accountability.
在 HN 中查看
doctorpangloss 2026/04/08 03:02
起毛？它是由聊天机器人编写的。
Fluff? It is written by a chatbot.
在 HN 中查看
minutesmith 2026/04/08 01:21 deleted
[已标记]
[flagged]
在 HN 中查看
syntaxing 2026/04/08 01:59
这就是为什么我是推动工程学科理念的大力支持者。与任何组织一样，纪律是自上而下的。自下而上只会引发灾难，并且是管理层和工程师之间目标不一致的明显迹象。
That’s why I’m a huge proponent of pushing the idea of engineering discipline. And like any organization, discipline comes from the top to bottom. Coming from bottom to top is just a recipe for disaster and clear tell sign of misaligned objectives between management and the engineers.
在 HN 中查看
roadbuster 2026/04/08 00:15
我认为 Clearmotion 拥有非常有趣的技术和产品（行驶稳定性），但让我们在这里描绘一个全貌：该公司成立于 2009 年，获得了 3.7 亿美元的资金，最近才获得大笔合同（2023 年与中国汽车制造商蔚来汽车签订了 10 亿美元的交易）。

我确信他们一直在为生存而奋斗，必须“快速行动”才能维持生计，但他们的技术已经酝酿了十多年。

I think Clearmotion has a very interesting technology and product (ride stabilization), but let's paint a full picture here: the company was founded in 2009, took on $370 million in funding, and only recently landed large contracts (a $1 billion dollar deal in 2023 with Chinese auto manufacturer, Nio).

I'm sure they were in a constant struggle for survival and had to "move fast" to stay afloat, but their technology is more than a decade in the making.

在 HN 中查看
MisterTea 2026/04/08 05:28
我大胆猜测，人们对增加不起眼的减震器的复杂性没什么兴趣。一个简单的被动机械系统被一个复杂的伺服机构所取代，该伺服机构具有传感器和执行器，这些伺服机构在汽车下的生活很艰难。

与被动系统相比，寿命有多长？维修与标准悬架的费用是多少？这对整体悬架设计有多大改变？它增加了多少重量？我敢打赌，自 2009 年以来这些问题的答案对汽车设计师或精算师来说根本没有吸引力。

就我个人而言，我不希望我的车上安装这样的系统。这听起来像是您必须处理的另一个昂贵的维护项目，但几乎没有增加任何价值。

I would venture a guess that there is little interest in adding complexity to the humble shock absorber. A simple passive mechanical system is replaced with a complex servomechanism featuring sensors and actuators which live a hard life under a car.

What is the longevity vs a passive system? How much is it to service vs standard suspension? How much does this change the overall suspension design? How much weight does it add? I bet the answers to those questions since 2009 were not at all enticing to automotive designers or bean counters.

Personally, I would not want such a system on my car. It sounds like another expensive maintenance item you have to deal with that adds little or no value.

在 HN 中查看
rryan 2026/04/08 00:51
我认为汽车行业的超长时间表使其很难取得成功。 ClearMotion 所取得的成就给我留下了深刻的印象
I think the super long timelines in automotive make it really hard to succeed. Really impressed with what ClearMotion accomplished given that
在 HN 中查看
bfeynman 2026/04/08 00:34
读得不错，但陷入了一个巨大的还原论陷阱，很多生存偏见伪装成设计哲学或战略赌注。 几十年前做出的决定的背景！=现在，人们在不同的约束下工作等等。试图将航空电子设备的例子描述为“减法”创新是最令人震惊的，晶体管小了1000倍以上，重量甚至都不是考虑因素。
Nice read but falls into a vast reductionist trap, a lot of survivorship bias dressed up as design philosophy or strategic bets. The context of decisions made decades ago != now, people were working under different constraints etc. Trying to frame the avionics example as the "subtractive" innovation is the most egregious, transistors were over 1000x times smaller, weight wasn't even a consideration.
在 HN 中查看
esseph 2026/04/08 02:11
> 许多生存偏见伪装成设计理念或战略赌注

我希望更多的人意识到这一点

> a lot of survivorship bias dressed up as design philosophy or strategic bets

I wish more people realized this

在 HN 中查看
AbrahamParangi 2026/04/08 00:02
我主要好奇的是收入有多少 实际的 ARR，即合同循环。对于硬件公司来说，拥有非凡的 ARR 是非常罕见的。
I'm mostly curious how much of that revenue is actual ARR, which is to say contractually recurring. It is pretty dang rare for a hardware company to have nontrivial ARR.
在 HN 中查看
zacka 2026/04/08 00:08
汽车合同通常以 6 年为周期，因此技术被设计到新车中，并被锁定到下一代汽车（5-7 年，具体取决于汽车制造商）。每年的销售额可能会有所波动，但相当可预测。
automotive contracts are typically on 6 year cycles, so tech gets designed into a new car and it's locked in until the next vehicle generation (5-7 years depending on the automaker). year to year sales can fluctuate but are fairly predictable.
在 HN 中查看
3dxsys 2026/04/07 23:16
成熟的外包，不确定的外包，这是非常正确的。许多初创公司恰恰外包了他们最不了解的系统部分，但这通常是最糟糕的决定。
Outsource the mature, insource the uncertain is very true. A lot of startups outsource precisely the part of the system they understand least, but that is oftentimes the worst possible decision.

As a human emdash enjoyer, I'm pretty damn grumpy that everyone assumes I'm a chatbot now. :P

JSR_FDED 2026/04/08 01:04
优化学习/迭代速度是一种可靠的方法，同样适用于软件开发。
Optimizing for learning/iteration speed is a solid approach that applies just as much to software development.

yrcyrc 2026/04/08 03:02

在 HN 中查看
nonameiguess 2026/04/08 00:41
根据该公司的维基百科文章草稿（由于尚未被认为足够值得注意而尚未发表），该公司已经存在了 17 年，于 2023 年建成了第一个生产设施，并于去年 3 月首次将工作单元交付到真实的汽车中。我觉得这并没有移动得那么快。它还表示，他们购买了本文中所批评的 Bose 产品是因为他们做错了事。

如果它有效，那就有效，但我真的希望公司和创始人在成功时能够诚实地说明他们如何以及为何成功，而不是一切都需要不断地投射所需的形象。

According to the draft Wikipedia article for this company that hasn't been published because it is not yet considered sufficiently noteworthy, this company has existed for 17 years, built their first production facility in 2023, and first delivered working units into real cars last March. That doesn't strike me as moving all that fast. It also says they acquired the Bose product they're shitting on in this article for doing things wrong.

If it works, it works, but I really wish companies and founders would just be honest about how and why they succeed when they do, instead of everything needing to be the constant projection of a desired image.

dcreater 2026/04/08 02:24
是的，这读起来像是事后修正主义营销内容，而不是工程师日记
Yup this reads like post hoc revisionist marketing content rather than an engineers diary

yalogin 2026/04/08 01:15
取决于您有多少资金、您的目标受众、目标垂直领域以及您的硬件的定制程度，不是吗？您可以削减和优化的东西只有这么多。
Depends on how much funding you have, your target audience, target vertical, and how custom your hardware is, no? There can only be so many things you can cut and optimize.

supliminal 2026/04/08 02:02
谁能用简单的英语告诉我们这家公司是做什么的？我只看到一件又一件的轶事，其中散布着令人感觉良好的侦探小说胡言乱语。我原本期待着飞机洞的比喻，但也许我很幸运，错过了。
Can someone in plain English please tell us what this company does? I just see one anecdote after another that’s scattered with feel-good whodathunkit babble. I was anticipating the airplane holes analogy, but maybe I got lucky and missed it.
